original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
problem_solved: With the listed routine patches inserted, the proof establishes the stronger statement that the realized parameters are exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\). As submitted, the lower bound is complete modulo routine leaf-graph/geometric details, and the upper bound is complete modulo the routine expansion of the Sadowsky-band construction and the PL secant-embeddedness lemma.
strategy_summary: The proof proceeds in two complementary parts. To rule out any realization for β≤√3, one considers a clean, Gβ-invariant triangulation of the parameter strip, constructs its quotient Möbius band and a connected “leaf” foliation by cevians whose affine images define a continuous loop of oriented support lines; a topological winding‐number argument then produces two leaves with perpendicular, intersecting support lines, and careful metric estimates force β>√3. Conversely, for each β>√3 one builds an explicit PL realization by starting from a scaled Sadowsky three-cylinder developable Möbius band, approximating it by a fine clean triangulation whose transverse edges are almost isometric while boundary edges contract, and then applying a slight affine contraction so that boundary edges shrink and transverse edges stretch, yielding the required embedded, Gβ-invariant map. Together these establish that exactly the interval (√3,∞) of parameters can be realized.
solution:
# Proof

We prove the slightly stronger statement that the realized numbers are exactly  
\[
(\sqrt 3,\infty).
\]
All lengths in the parameter strip or in its quotient are Euclidean lengths in the flat metric.

We shall use one classical outside input in the upper-bound construction: Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction of embedded developable Möbius bands, in the quantitative form stated below. Everything else is proved directly.

---

## 1. The lower bound: no \(\beta\le \sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>0\), write \(G=G_\beta\), and suppose that \(\beta\) is realized by a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\). We prove \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

### 1.1. Elementary facts about clean triangulations

A clean triangle has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\): since its three vertices lie on the two boundary lines \(y=0\) and \(y=1\), exactly two lie on one boundary component, and the edge joining those two is the unique boundary edge. The other two edges join the two boundary components; call them transverse edges.

We first record the following elementary structure lemma.

**Lemma 1.** The triangulation is face-to-face in the following sense:

1. two distinct edges have disjoint relative interiors;
2. every transverse edge is incident to exactly two triangles;
3. every boundary edge is incident to exactly one triangle.

**Proof.** Two transverse edges cannot cross transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\). Indeed, near an interior point of such a crossing, each of the two triangles containing those edges occupies one of the open half-disks determined by its edge. Two open half-disks bounded by crossing lines always overlap in an open sector, contradicting disjointness of triangle interiors.

If two transverse edges are collinear and overlap in more than one point, then they are the same edge, because a non-horizontal line meets each of the two boundary lines \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in exactly one point.

Boundary edges cannot overlap in a nontrivial interval: at an interior point of such an overlap, both adjacent triangles contain an inward half-neighborhood of the boundary, again forcing overlapping interiors. Similarly, an endpoint of a transverse edge cannot lie in the relative interior of a boundary edge, because the triangle with the boundary edge contains a small inward half-disk there, while the triangle with the transverse edge occupies a nonempty sector inside that half-disk.

Thus distinct triangles meet only in common faces. Now let \(e\) be a transverse edge and let \(z\) be an interior point of \(e\). A small disk centered at \(z\) is divided by \(e\) into two sides. At most one triangle can occupy each side, by disjointness of interiors, and at least one triangle must occupy each side, because the triangles cover \(\Sigma\). Hence \(e\) is incident to exactly two triangles. The same argument with a half-disk at an interior point of a boundary edge shows that a boundary edge is incident to exactly one triangle. ∎

---

### 1.2. Passing to the quotient Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
M_\beta=[0,\beta]\times[0,1]/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
so \(M_\beta\) is a compact Möbius band. Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because the realization condition gives \(f(Gp)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
Moreover, if \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition \(p=G^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need that the quotient triangulation is finite. First observe that \(f\) is injective on each triangle. Indeed, if \(f|_\tau\) had affine rank \(<2\), then there would be distinct points \(p,q\in\tau\), arbitrarily close to each other, with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would give \(p=G^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\). But every nontrivial \(G^k\) moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\): for even \(k=2n\), it translates horizontally by \(2n\beta\), and for odd \(k=2n+1\), it changes the \(x\)-coordinate by \((2n+1)\beta\). This is impossible for \(p,q\) sufficiently close.

Therefore \(f|_\tau\) has rank \(2\), and hence is injective on \(\tau\). It follows that no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. For if \(z\in \tau\cap G^k\tau\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\tau\), so
\[
f(z)=f(G^k w)=f(w).
\]
Since \(z,w\in\tau\) and \(f|_\tau\) is injective, we get \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the \(G\)-action unless \(k=0\).

A compact fundamental rectangle, for instance \([0,2\beta]\times[0,1]\), meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness. Every \(G\)-orbit of triangles has a representative meeting this rectangle. Hence the induced triangulation of \(M_\beta\) is finite. By Lemma 1, it is a finite face-to-face clean triangulation of the quotient Möbius band.

---

### 1.3. A cevian-lengthening lemma

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(0\le s\le1\), put
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1.
\]
The segment \([a,r(s)]\) is a cevian of \(\Delta\). We claim that every such segment is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two transverse edges are strictly lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Because \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)
=
2\Bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\Bigr).
\]
The boundary edge is strictly shortened, so \(q''(s)<0\). Thus \(q\) is strictly concave. A concave function lies above its chord, hence
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Therefore
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|
\]
for every \(s\in[0,1]\). In particular, the image of every such cevian is a nondegenerate straight segment.

---

### 1.4. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider the one-parameter family of cevians joining the apex to the opposite boundary edge. We call these segments the leaves of the triangle. The endpoint leaves are precisely the two transverse edges.

For the finite quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\), define a graph \(\Lambda_M\) as follows. Each triangle contributes one closed interval, parametrizing its leaves. Whenever two triangles share a transverse edge, glue the corresponding endpoints of their intervals. By Lemma 1, every transverse edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\).

We claim that \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all leaves represented by points of \(C\). Equivalently, \(U_C\) is the union of all triangles whose leaf intervals lie in \(C\).

We show that \(U_C\) is open in \(M_\beta\). This is clear at interior points of triangles and at interior points of boundary edges. At an interior point of a transverse edge, the two incident triangles lie in the same component of \(\Lambda_M\), because their leaf intervals are glued at the endpoint representing that transverse edge.

It remains to consider boundary vertices. Let \(v\) be a boundary vertex. Choose a sufficiently small half-disk in \(M_\beta\) centered at \(v\), meeting only triangles incident to \(v\). Radial projection from \(v\) sends the incident triangle sectors to a finite subdivision of a semicircle. Consecutive sectors share an edge emanating from \(v\). Except for the two boundary rays at the ends of the semicircle, these shared edges are transverse edges, so consecutive incident triangles are connected in the leaf graph. Hence all triangles incident to \(v\) lie in the same component of \(\Lambda_M\). Thus \(U_C\) contains a neighborhood of each of its boundary vertices.

Therefore every \(U_C\) is open. Its complement is a union of the corresponding open sets for the other components. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, there is only one component. Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. A finite connected graph in which every vertex has valence \(2\) is a circle:
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Now build the analogous leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) upstairs in \(\Sigma\). The same local argument shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The transformation \(G\) acts on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), and the quotient graph is \(\Lambda_M\).

We next show that no nontrivial power of \(G\) fixes a lifted leaf. Write a lifted leaf as the segment with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
If \(k=2n\) is even, then \(G^k\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\). Thus \(G^k\) can fix the leaf only if \(2n\beta=0\), i.e. \(k=0\).

If \(k=2n+1\) is odd, then \(G^k\) swaps the two boundary components. For \(G^k\) to fix the leaf as a set, we would need simultaneously
\[
x+t=x+(2n+1)\beta
\]
and
\[
x=x+t+(2n+1)\beta.
\]
Thus
\[
t=(2n+1)\beta
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
t=-(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Therefore the \(G\)-action on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is free.

Thus \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) is a connected infinite covering. Consequently
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong \mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from the boundary component \(y=0\) to the boundary component \(y=1\). By the cevian-lengthening lemma, its image is a nondegenerate straight segment, and therefore determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

If \(\ell\) is a lifted leaf, then \(G\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has the same image segment with the opposite orientation. Indeed, \(G\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G=f\).

Hence, along any fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G\ell\), the oriented support line travels from some oriented line to the same unoriented line with the opposite orientation.

---

### 1.5. A lemma about paths of oriented lines

We need the following topological fact.

**Lemma 2.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\). Suppose \(L_1\) is the same unoriented line as \(L_0\), but with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters \(s_1,s_2\) such that the corresponding unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. This is independent of the choice of \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends
\[
(u,m)\mapsto(-u,-m).
\]

Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(L_s\), and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
and
\[
b(s,\delta)
=
u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),
\qquad
(a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
we have
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose, for contradiction, that \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then normalization gives a continuous map
\[
\Phi:S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1,
\qquad
\Phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}.
\]
On \(\delta=0\), \(\Phi\) is constant. But on the middle circle \(\delta=\frac12\), the relation above gives
\[
\Phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)
=
-\Phi\left(s,\frac12\right).
\]
A map \(\psi:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(\psi(s+\frac12)=-\psi(s)\) has odd degree: after lifting \(\psi\) to an angle function, the angle changes over a half-period by an odd multiple of \(\pi\), and hence over a full period by an odd multiple of \(2\pi\). But \(\Phi|_{\delta=1/2}\) is homotopic through the cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1/2]\) to the constant map \(\Phi|_{\delta=0}\), so it must have degree \(0\). This contradiction proves that \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere.

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says that the two directions are perpendicular. Let the corresponding oriented lines be represented by
\[
(u,p\times u)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
(v,q\times v).
\]
Then
\[
\begin{aligned}
b
&=
u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)  \\
&=
(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\end{aligned}
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the condition \(b=0\) says that the two nonparallel lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. ∎

Applying Lemma 2 to the path of oriented support lines along a fundamental interval of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), we obtain two distinct leaves in the quotient \(M_\beta\), call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at some point
\[
P\in\mathbb R^3.
\]

---

### 1.6. Geometry of lifted leaves

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

We first prove that
\[
|t|<\beta. \tag{3}
\]

If \(t=\beta\), then the two endpoints of \(J\) are identified by \(G\), so their images under \(f\) coincide. But the image of \(J\) is a nondegenerate segment, contradiction. Similarly, \(t=-\beta\) is impossible.

Suppose \(t>\beta\). Translating horizontally, assume \(J\) has endpoints \((0,0)\) and \((t,1)\). Then \(GJ\) has endpoints \((\beta,1)\) and \((t+\beta,0)\). The segments \(J\) and \(GJ\) intersect at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(p,q\in J\) with \(p=Gq\). Hence \(f(p)=f(q)\), contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate segment \(J\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is identical, using \(G^{-1}\). This proves (3).

Now parametrize the boundary circle of \(M_\beta\) by a coordinate modulo \(2\beta\) as follows:

- a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\);
- a top point \((v,1)\) has coordinate \(v-\beta\).

For a leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1),
\]
the two boundary arcs between its endpoints therefore have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\beta+t. \tag{4}
\]
Both are positive by (3).

We also need the following boundary-length observation. The quotient triangulation is finite, so the boundary circle is divided into finitely many boundary edges. On each boundary edge \(e\), the affine map \(F|_e\) has contraction factor \(\lambda_e<1\). Therefore every nontrivial boundary arc \(\gamma\) is strictly shortened:
\[
\ell_F(\gamma)<\ell_0(\gamma). \tag{5}
\]
Indeed, decompose \(\gamma\) into finitely many subsegments \(I_j\) lying in boundary edges \(e_j\). Then
\[
\ell_F(\gamma)
=
\sum_j \lambda_{e_j}|I_j|
<
\sum_j |I_j|
=
\ell_0(\gamma).
\]

---

### 1.7. The metric estimate

Recall the two quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\).

The relative interiors of distinct leaves in \(M_\beta\) are disjoint. Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. Hence at least one of the two image segments lies entirely in a closed ray from \(P\) along its support line. Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\), the farther endpoint satisfies
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the cevian-lengthening lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Therefore, setting
\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T)),
\]
we have
\[
y>1. \tag{6}
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Replacing this lift by its \(G\)-translate changes \(t\) to \(-t\), and therefore interchanges the two quantities \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\) from (4). Thus we may choose the lift so that the boundary arc containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\beta+t.
\]
Call this arc \(D\), and call the other boundary arc \(H\). Then
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\qquad
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Let
\[
A,C
\]
be the endpoints of the segment \(F(T)\), and write
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
By the cevian-lengthening lemma applied to \(T\),
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{7}
\]

The arc \(H\) connects the endpoints of \(F(T)\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge L.
\]
Using the strict boundary shortening (5),
\[
\beta-t
=
\ell_0(H)
>
\ell_F(H)
\ge
L
>
\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{8}
\]

Now the other boundary arc \(D\) also connects \(A\) to \(C\), and it passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
The point \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), where \(y>1\) by (6). Among all points at distance \(y\) from the line through \(A\) and \(C\), the quantity \(|A-Q|+|Q-C|\) is minimized when the perpendicular projection of \(Q\) to that line is the midpoint of \(AC\). Hence
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
\ge
\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using (6) and (7),
\[
\ell_F(D)
>
\sqrt{(1+t^2)+4}
=
\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
By strict boundary shortening again,
\[
\beta+t
=
\ell_0(D)
>
\ell_F(D)
>
\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{9}
\]

Combining (8) and (9),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}
\ge
\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply, after squaring,
\[
t<\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]
while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply
\[
t>\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
This is impossible. Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Thus no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## 2. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

We now prove the converse. The construction uses the classical Sadowsky--Wunderlich family of embedded developable Möbius bands.

### 2.1. Sadowsky bands

We use the following standard form of the Sadowsky construction.

**Lemma 3.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists a map
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with the following properties:

1. \(E_\mu(X+\mu,1-Y)=E_\mu(X,Y)\);
2. the induced quotient map
   \[
   \bigl(\mathbb R\times[0,1]\bigr)/\langle (X,Y)\mapsto(X+\mu,1-Y)\rangle
   \longrightarrow
   \mathbb R^3
   \]
   is embedded;
3. the quotient surface is a \(C^{1,1}\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), developable Möbius band;
4. it is foliated by full-width straight rulings, and \(E_\mu\) maps each ruling affinely and isometrically to a straight segment;
5. the two boundary curves are parametrized by arclength in the \(X\)-coordinate;
6. the construction extends to a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) ruled band.

**Justification.** This is the classical three-cylinder construction of Sadowsky, later analyzed by Wunderlich \(\cite{Sad30,Wun62}\). In that construction the surface consists of three planar pieces and three cylindrical pieces, joined along common generators with matching tangent planes. The strip is bent isometrically, so boundary arclength and ruling length are preserved, and the rulings map to straight line segments.

The elementary non-overlap computation in the three-cylinder construction gives the following quantitative form. If the Möbius length parameter is \(\mu\) and the auxiliary cylinder diameter is \(d>0\), then the largest allowable half-width is
\[
b_{\max}
=
\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}
-
\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Since \(\mu>\sqrt3\), we can choose
\[
0<d<
\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3},
\]
which makes \(b_{\max}>\frac12\). Hence a strip of width \(1\) lies strictly inside the embedded range. Because the inequality is strict, the same construction remains embedded for a slightly wider strip. This gives the required \(E_\mu\). ∎

---

### 2.2. A PL approximation lemma

Let \(c,L>0\), and define
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]
on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\).

We need to approximate a smooth ruled embedded band by a PL ruled band whose transverse edges are almost as long as in the parameter strip and whose boundary edges are not expanded.

**Lemma 4.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be the lift of one of the scaled Sadowsky bands from Lemma 3, invariant under \(H_{L,c}\). Suppose its full-width rulings are
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
Fix \(x_0\), put
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,
\]
and let
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
Then for every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) if and only if \(P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\);
2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded:
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\le |P-Q|;
   \]
3. every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Because the rulings form a noncrossing foliation of the strip by full-width segments, \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a strictly increasing homeomorphism.

The map \(H_{L,c}\) sends \(R_x\) to \(R_{\phi(x)}\), where
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Indeed, \(H_{L,c}\) sends the lower endpoint \((x,0)\) to the upper endpoint \((x+L,c)\), and sends the upper endpoint \((h(x),c)\) to the lower endpoint \((h(x)+L,0)\). Thus the image ruling has lower endpoint \(h(x)+L\). Therefore
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L,
\]
and hence
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L. \tag{10}
\]

We also have
\[
|h(x)-x|<L. \tag{11}
\]
If \(h(x)-x=L\), then the two endpoints of \(R_x\) are identified by \(H_{L,c}\), contradicting the fact that \(E\) maps \(R_x\) isometrically to a nondegenerate segment. If \(h(x)-x>L\), then \(R_x\) intersects \(H_{L,c}(R_x)\) in an interior point of the strip. Thus two distinct points of the same ruling would be identified by \(H_{L,c}\) and hence have the same image under \(E\), again contradicting injectivity on that ruling. The case \(h(x)-x<-L\) is identical, using \(H_{L,c}^{-1}\). This proves (11). In particular,
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L>x.
\]

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,
\qquad
2\rho<\varepsilon. \tag{12}
\]
Choose a partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
fine enough that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\qquad
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for every \(i\), and fine enough that all seams between the planar and cylindrical pieces of the Sadowsky band occur among the rulings \(R_{x_i}\). We will impose one further fineness condition below to guarantee embeddedness.

Extend the sequence by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i)
\]
for all \(i\in\mathbb Z\). Then by (10),
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Put
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),
\qquad
T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The four points
\[
B_i,\ B_{i+1},\ T_{i+1},\ T_i
\]
bound a clean quadrilateral \(Q_i\). These quadrilaterals tile \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\).

For \(0\le i<N\), triangulate \(Q_i\) by the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), and then extend this choice \(H_{L,c}\)-invariantly. Thus the diagonal choice alternates under one application of \(H_{L,c}\) and repeats under \(H_{L,c}^2\). The result is an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),
\qquad
F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. Since \(E\) is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant and the triangulation is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant, the map \(F_0\) is also \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant.

Boundary edges are nonexpanded because their images are chords of boundary arcs of \(E\), and the boundary curves of \(E\) are parametrized by arclength.

Now consider transverse edges. First, a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\) is mapped isometrically, so
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Next consider a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Set
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,
\qquad
r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=
|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge
|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&>
r-\rho \\
&>
d-2\rho \\
&>
d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other possible diagonal type is handled in the same way, using the lower boundary increment \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\). Thus every transverse edge is shortened by less than \(\varepsilon\).

The horizontal span of a ruling edge is \(|h(x_i)-x_i|\), which is at most \(S\) on one fundamental interval and hence everywhere by (10). The horizontal span of a diagonal is at most \(S+\rho\). By (12), every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

It remains to choose the partition fine enough so that the quotient PL map is embedded. Let
\[
N_{L,c}=\bigl(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\bigr)/\langle H_{L,c}\rangle
\]
be the quotient Möbius band. By Lemma 3, the smooth ruled band extends to a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) band. Thus the compact quotient \(N_{L,c}\) sits in the interior of a compact embedded \(C^{1,1}\) surface \(\widehat N\). Such a compact \(C^{1,1}\) embedded surface has a tubular neighborhood: there is a neighborhood \(U\) of \(\widehat E(\widehat N)\) and a continuous nearest-point projection
\[
\pi:U\to \widehat E(\widehat N).
\]

As the partition mesh tends to \(0\), the secant PL maps converge uniformly to \(E\), and after projecting by \(\pi\), they converge to the identity in first derivatives on each smooth piece. On planar pieces this is exact. On cylindrical pieces it is the usual chord approximation estimate. Because all seams are included among the partition rulings and the tangent planes match across seams, the convergence is uniform on the quotient.

Thus, for sufficiently fine partition, \(F_0(N_{L,c})\subset U\), and the map
\[
P=\widehat E^{-1}\circ\pi\circ F_0:N_{L,c}\to \widehat N
\]
is uniformly \(C^1\)-close, piece by piece, to the inclusion \(N_{L,c}\hookrightarrow\widehat N\).

For such a sufficiently small \(C^1\)-error, \(P\) is injective. Indeed, cover \(N_{L,c}\) by finitely many coordinate disks with convex coordinate images. If two points lie in a common coordinate disk, then along the coordinate segment joining them, the derivative of \(P\) differs from the identity by less than, say, \(1/2\), so \(P\) cannot identify them. For pairs of points not lying in a common coordinate disk, compactness gives a positive lower bound on their distance in \(N_{L,c}\); sufficiently small uniform \(C^0\)-closeness to the inclusion prevents such pairs from being identified. Hence \(P\) is injective.

If the quotient map induced by \(F_0\) identified two points, then \(P\) would also identify them. Therefore the quotient map induced by \(F_0\) is embedded. Lifting back to \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\), we obtain
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). ∎

---

### 2.3. Realizing every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Let \(E_\mu\) be the width-one, length-\(\mu\) Sadowsky band from Lemma 3. For this band, choose a fundamental interval of rulings and define
\[
S_\mu=\max |h(x)-x|
\]
over that interval. Choose
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

We now choose a horizontal contraction factor \(a<1\), close to \(1\). We require
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]
Set
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu},
\qquad
L=a\beta.
\]
Then
\[
c>1,
\qquad
L=c\mu.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by the factor \(c\). This gives a width-\(c\), length-\(L\) embedded ruled band invariant under
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
The ruling-span bound scales from \(S_\mu\) to \(cS_\mu\), so \(cD_\mu\) is an admissible span bound.

We choose \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\) so that
\[
m:=
\min_{0\le u\le D_\mu}
\left[
c\sqrt{1+u^2}
-
\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}
\right]
>0. \tag{13}
\]
This is possible because when \(a=1\), we have \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and then
\[
c\sqrt{1+u^2}
>
\sqrt{c^2u^2+1}
\]
for every \(u\). The minimum over the compact interval \([0,D_\mu]\) is therefore positive, and positivity persists for all \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\).

Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]
Apply Lemma 4 to the scaled band with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]
We obtain an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
satisfying the four conclusions of Lemma 4.

Now define the affine map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Because \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
A(x+\beta,1-y)
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Set
\[
f=F_0\circ A,
\qquad
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable, locally finite, \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on each triangle.

We verify the squeeze inequalities.

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Since \(A\) multiplies boundary lengths by \(a\), and \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\le
|Ap-Aq|
=
a|p-q|
<
|p-q|.
\]
Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(s\) be the horizontal span of the edge \(A(p)A(q)\) in the height-\(c\) strip. By Lemma 4,
\[
s<cD_\mu.
\]
Write
\[
s=cu
\]
with
\[
0\le u<D_\mu.
\]
Then
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|
=
\sqrt{s^2+c^2}
=
c\sqrt{1+u^2}.
\]
On the other hand, since \(A\) scales horizontally by \(a\) and vertically by \(c\), the original edge \(pq\) has length
\[
|p-q|
=
\sqrt{\left(\frac{s}{a}\right)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}.
\]
Using Lemma 4 and the choice \(\varepsilon<m\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
|f(p)-f(q)|
&=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)| \\
&\ge
|A(p)-A(q)|-\varepsilon \\
&=
c\sqrt{1+u^2}-\varepsilon \\
&>
\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1} \\
&=
|p-q|.
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally, for any \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow
Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining this with the lower bound, the realized numbers are exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Hence the infimum of the set of realized numbers is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]

---
